citation: Stemper BD, Shah AS, Pintar FA, et al. Drill-Specific Head Impacts in Collegiate Football Practice: Implications for Reducing 'Friendly Fire' Exposure. Ann Biomed Eng. 2017;45(12):2648-2662. doi: 10.1007/s10439-017-1898-y
abstract: This study quantified head impact biomechanics by specific drill type in collegiate football practice to identify which drills contribute most to cumulative head impact exposure. Head impact data were collected using helmet-mounted accelerometers during practices across a collegiate football season. Drills were categorized and head impact frequency and magnitude were compared across drill types to identify opportunities for reducing practice-based head impact exposure.
tldr: Not all football practice drills carry equal concussion risk. This study found that certain full-contact drills — particularly those involving teammate-on-teammate ('friendly fire') contact — account for a disproportionate share of head impacts during practice. Coaches can meaningfully reduce cumulative head impact exposure by limiting or modifying these specific high-impact drills without eliminating overall football preparation.
methods: Helmet-mounted accelerometer systems recorded head impact kinematics in collegiate football players across an entire practice season. Each impact was tagged to a specific drill type. Linear and rotational accelerations were analyzed by drill category, and cumulative exposure metrics were calculated. Drills were classified to identify those generating the most frequent and highest-magnitude impacts.
findings: Certain contact-based drills contributed disproportionately to cumulative head impact burden. Teammate-on-teammate contact ('friendly fire') drills generated significant impact exposure. High-magnitude impacts were concentrated in a subset of drill types, suggesting that targeted drill modification could substantially reduce overall practice head impact exposure.
limitations: The study was conducted at a single institution, limiting generalizability across programs with different practice schemes. Accelerometer-based systems may not perfectly replicate true head kinematics. Drills were self-reported or observed by research staff, which may introduce classification inconsistency. The study did not directly link drill-specific impacts to concussion outcomes.
practicalImplications: Football programs can reduce cumulative head impact exposure by identifying and modifying or eliminating high-impact 'friendly fire' drills from practice schedules. Staff should audit their practice script to quantify which drills generate the most head impacts and consider substituting equivalent skill-development activities with lower contact intensity, consistent with evolving NCAA and NFL practice contact limitations.
athleteDev: Skill development can be maintained by replacing high-contact friendly-fire drills with technique-focused or thud-level alternatives. This is particularly relevant for linemen and linebackers who absorb the highest practice impact loads. Periodizing full-contact drill exposure across the season — heaviest early, tapering mid-season — can preserve development while protecting long-term neurological health.
rtp: Quantifying drill-specific impact exposure provides a framework for progressive return-to-practice protocols after concussion. Players returning from head injury can be reintegrated beginning with no-contact and drill-contact activities, avoiding the high-impact friendly-fire drills identified in this study until fully cleared, reducing re-injury risk during the vulnerable post-concussion window.

citation: Benson EM, Elphingstone JW, Paul KD, Schick S, Shihab YA, Barlow D, Ponce BA, Brabston EW, Momaya AM. Eccentric Hamstring Strength Imbalance among Football and Soccer Athletes. South Med J. 2024;117(4):214-219. doi: 10.14423/SMJ.0000000000001674
abstract: This cross-sectional study compared eccentric hamstring strength imbalances across 631 soccer and American football athletes at high school, collegiate, and professional levels using the NordBord Hamstring Testing System. The study measured maximum hamstring force, torque, and work imbalances between limbs. High school soccer athletes demonstrated significantly greater hamstring imbalances than football athletes, and imbalances decreased with advancing skill level in soccer but not in football.
tldr: High school soccer athletes show substantially greater interlimb eccentric hamstring imbalances than football athletes, placing them at elevated risk for hamstring strain injury. Imbalances improve with age and competitive level in soccer (likely reflecting more structured eccentric training), but football athletes show no significant change across skill levels. Single-sport specialization in high school is associated with greater work imbalances than multisport participation. Football programs that already emphasize weight training may be inadvertently protecting players from hamstring asymmetry.
methods: Cross-sectional study enrolling 631 athletes during preseason 2021, including high school (n=459), collegiate (n=149), and professional (n=24) male soccer and football players plus female soccer athletes. Nordic hamstring exercise was performed on the NordBord device. Maximum force (N), torque (N·m), and work (N·s) imbalances between legs were computed. One-way ANOVA with LSD post-hoc tests and chi-square tests compared groups; statistical significance set at P<0.05.
findings: High school soccer players showed significantly greater torque imbalance (P=0.03) and work imbalance (P<0.01) than football players. Male soccer athletes at high school had significantly higher maximum force (P=0.018), torque (P=0.035), and work (P=0.033) imbalances than college/professional counterparts. Female high school soccer players had significantly higher torque (P=0.045) and work (P=0.001) imbalances than collegiate females. Football athletes showed no significant imbalance changes across skill levels. Single-sport high school athletes had significantly greater work imbalances than multisport athletes (9.1% vs 7.0%, P=0.012). HSI rates were higher in soccer (11.7%) than football (4.5%, P=0.004).
limitations: Pre-measurement activity was not controlled. Imbalances were not stratified by field position due to insufficient statistical power. Cross-sectional design prevents causal conclusions about whether improved balance results from skill-level advancement or self-selection. Study was conducted at limited institutions and may not generalize broadly.
practicalImplications: Football strength and conditioning staff should incorporate eccentric hamstring testing (NordBord or similar) as a screening tool, particularly for high school athletes and positions requiring explosive acceleration/deceleration (DBs, WRs). Force imbalances exceeding 20% should trigger targeted single-leg eccentric programming. Nordic curl protocols have demonstrated 50-70% reduction in initial HSI risk and 85% reduction in reinjury rates and should be considered standard injury prevention for all skill-position players.
athleteDev: Early enrollment of football players in structured weight training — already common in most programs — appears protective against eccentric hamstring imbalance. Multisport participation in high school is associated with better hamstring balance and should be encouraged where possible. For positions with high sprint demands (DBs, WRs, RBs), single-leg deadlifts, Nordic curls, and single-leg Romanian chair holds should be integrated into year-round strength programs to address asymmetry proactively.
rtp: Athletes returning from hamstring strain injury should be cleared using NordBord testing to confirm limb symmetry before full sport return. Work imbalance >20% between legs indicates elevated re-injury risk (reported recurrence rates of 14-34%). A progressive eccentric loading protocol — including Nordic curls — should be completed before RTP clearance, given the 85% reinjury risk reduction demonstrated with this training approach.

citation: Pfaller AY, Brooks MA, Hetzel S, McGuine TA. Effect of a New Rule Limiting Full Contact Practice on the Incidence of Sport-Related Concussion in High School Football Players. Am J Sports Med. 2019;47(10):2294-2299. doi: 10.1177/0363546519860120
abstract: This cohort study examined whether a Wisconsin Interscholastic Athletic Association (WIAA) rule change limiting full-contact practice duration reduced sport-related concussion (SRC) rates in high school football. Data from 2081 players in pre-rule seasons (2012-2013) were compared to 945 players in the post-rule 2014 season. Athletic trainers tracked athlete exposures and concussion incidence across practice and competition settings.
tldr: Limiting full-contact practice time by rule reduced high school football practice concussions by 57%, with no corresponding increase in game-time concussions. This is strong evidence that targeted contact restrictions during practice are effective for concussion reduction without compromising game-readiness or technique. The data also show that overall concussion rates trended down (1.58 to 1.28 per 1000 AEs), though the overall change was not statistically significant, likely due to sample size. For football programs at any level, reducing unnecessary full-contact repetitions in practice is one of the most evidence-backed interventions available.
methods: Prospective cohort study across Wisconsin high schools: 34 schools in 2012, 18 in 2013 (pre-rule), and 26 schools in 2014 (post-rule). Athletic trainers classified practice sessions as full-contact, drill-contact, or no-contact, and recorded all athlete exposures (AEs) and SRC diagnoses. SRC defined per American Academy of Neurology guidelines. Chi-square tests compared concussion rates; Wilcoxon rank sum tests compared days lost.
findings: Practice SRC rate fell 57% after the rule change: from 0.76 per 1000 AEs (2012-2013) to 0.33 per 1000 AEs in 2014 (P=0.003). Game concussion rate was unchanged (5.81 vs 5.74 per 1000 AEs, P=0.999). Overall concussion rate trended down from 1.58 to 1.28 per 1000 AEs but was not statistically significant (P=0.139). Days lost to SRC were not significantly different (13 vs 14 days, P=0.967). No difference in concussion rates by prior football experience, suggesting less-experienced players were not harmed by reduced contact practice.
limitations: Head impact magnitude was not directly measured, so volume reduction in total head impacts is inferred. Classification of practice contact type by athletic trainers may introduce bias. Study was underpowered to detect significant overall concussion rate change. Other injury types were not tracked. Adherence to the new rule was assumed but not directly verified. Time-series design is susceptible to secular trends including changing concussion awareness and clinical practice.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football programs should use this evidence to support limiting unnecessary full-contact repetitions in practice, consistent with existing NFLPA collective bargaining agreement restrictions on padded practices. The argument that full contact in practice is required to prevent injury during games is not supported by this data — game concussion rates did not rise after contact was restricted. Programs should audit their practice scripts to classify contact levels and minimize full-contact time to what is strictly necessary for competitive preparation.
athleteDev: Players at all experience levels — including those with less prior football experience — showed no disadvantage from reduced full-contact practice, suggesting that technical skill development is preserved with drill-contact and thud-level activities. Programs should develop practice structures that achieve necessary positional skill development within no-contact and drill-contact frameworks as much as possible, reserving full-contact for specific installation and game-simulation periods.
rtp: The study indirectly informs RTP protocols by demonstrating that concussed players who miss practice but return to game competition do not face elevated concussion risk when the practice environment has reduced contact levels. Graduated RTP progressions that begin with no-contact and drill-contact activities are consistent with the practice structure that reduced overall concussion incidence in this study.

citation: Murphy CP, Frangiamore SJ, Mannava S, Sanchez A, Beiter ER, Whalen JM, Price MD, LaPrade RF, Provencher MT. Effect of Anterior Glenoid Labral Tears and Glenoid Bone Loss at the NFL Combine on Future NFL Performance. Orthop J Sports Med. 2018;6(7):2325967118784884. doi: 10.1177/2325967118784884
abstract: This retrospective cohort study reviewed all 2285 NFL Combine participants from 2009 to 2015 to determine whether MRI-confirmed anterior glenoid labral tears and associated injuries affected future NFL performance. Of 2285 players, 206 (9%) had anterior labral tears confirmed by MRI; 908 players without surgery history or significant missed time served as controls. NFL draft position, games played, games started, and snap percentage across the first two NFL seasons were compared between injured players and controls.
tldr: Having an anterior labral tear (Bankart lesion) confirmed at the NFL Combine does not independently reduce games played, games started, or snap percentage in a player's first two NFL seasons. However, labral tears occurring alongside other shoulder injuries (Hill-Sachs, SLAP, glenoid bone loss, rotator cuff tear) significantly drop a player's draft position — meaning teams penalize these athletes in the draft even though the combined injuries don't appear to hurt on-field performance. Glenoid bone loss specifically lowered draft position but did not affect playing time. Surgical versus non-surgical management showed no performance differences. This data is relevant for evaluating college athletes at the NFL Combine and making clearance and draft decisions.
methods: Retrospective cohort study of NFL Combine participants 2009-2015. MRI scans reviewed independently by two fellowship-trained orthopaedic surgeons for anterior labral tears, associated injuries (SLAP, glenoid bone loss, Hill-Sachs, rotator cuff tear, HAGL, combined posterior tears), and surgical evidence. Control group: players with no surgery history and no more than 2 missed collegiate games. Performance metrics (draft position, games played, games started, year 1 and year 2 snap %) collected from NFL.com and Pro Football Focus. Mann-Whitney U tests, chi-square, and Fisher exact tests used for comparisons.
findings: 206 of 2285 players (9%) had MRI-confirmed anterior labral tears; 20 were bilateral (226 total affected shoulders). Players with anterior labral tears did not perform significantly worse than controls in draft position (P=0.259), games played (P=0.391), games started (P=0.486), or snap percentage year 1 (P=0.268) or year 2 (P=0.757). Concomitant injuries significantly worsened draft position vs isolated labral tears (170.1 vs 95.8 pick, P=0.003). Glenoid bone loss reduced draft position (175.5 vs 95.8, P=0.001) but not playing metrics. Severity of bone loss did not significantly affect any metric. Operative vs nonoperative management showed no significant performance differences.
limitations: Retrospective design; not all players underwent shoulder MRI at the Combine, potentially missing asymptomatic tears. MR angiography and 3D CT were not consistently available, limiting sensitivity for certain lesions and bone loss quantification. Physical examination findings were not correlated to imaging. Confounding variables (collegiate performance, combine testing, interview, team needs) could affect draft position. Follow-up limited to first 2 NFL seasons; some players had only 2-year data available.
practicalImplications: NFL and college medical staffs should recognize that an anterior labral tear — even with associated injuries — does not independently predict reduced on-field performance in the first two years of professional play. Teams may be over-penalizing athletes in the draft for labral findings that do not translate to playing time differences. For collegiate athletes being evaluated for professional opportunities, appropriate surgical management of instability before the Combine does not appear to disadvantage performance outcomes relative to nonoperative care.
athleteDev: Athletes with anterior labral tears or a history of shoulder instability can be developed for professional play with appropriate management. The 9% prevalence of MRI-confirmed labral tears at the Combine reflects the high contact demands of collegiate football. Strength and conditioning programs emphasizing rotator cuff and scapular stabilizer conditioning may reduce recurrence risk for athletes returning from labral injury. Positions with highest prevalence (defensive backs, defensive linemen, linebackers) warrant proactive shoulder screening.
rtp: The lack of significant performance differences between operatively and nonoperatively managed labral tears suggests both pathways can lead to successful return to elite competition. Players returning from anterior labral repair should follow validated progressive RTP protocols with attention to end-range external rotation loading before clearing for full-contact practice. The presence of concomitant injuries (Hill-Sachs, glenoid bone loss) warrants more conservative RTP timelines and may require surgical stabilization to reduce recurrent dislocation risk in contact sport athletes.

citation: Reams N, Hayward RA, Kutcher JS, Burke JF. Effect of Concussion on Performance of National Football League Players. Int J Sports Physiol Perform. 2017;12(8):1100-1106. doi: 10.1123/ijspp.2016-0508
abstract: This retrospective cohort study examined 1,882 NFL skill-position players (running backs, quarterbacks, wide receivers, tight ends) from 2007-2010 to determine whether concussion affects on-field performance as measured by Defense-Adjusted Yards Above Replacement (DYAR). Players reported on the weekly NFL injury report with concussion were compared to controls with other head/neck injuries on performance changes relative to each player's baseline. Fixed-effects regression models estimated performance before and after injury.
tldr: NFL skill-position players who were cleared to return to play after concussion performed at their pre-injury baseline level — there was no measurable decline in game performance upon return. However, players showed significantly reduced DYAR performance in the one and two weeks prior to their concussion appearing on the official injury report, which may indicate delayed diagnosis, underreporting, or a multi-hit cumulative injury phenomenon. For performance and medical staff, this suggests current return-to-play criteria are capturing functional recovery adequately, but earlier detection of subclinical concussion effects may be an important opportunity to improve athlete protection.
methods: Retrospective cohort study of 1,882 NFL skill-position players over 2007-2010 regular seasons. Concussion identified via weekly NFL injury report. Controls: players with other head/neck injuries. Primary outcome: Defense-Adjusted Yards Above Replacement (DYAR) from Football Outsiders, a context-adjusted performance metric. Multi-level fixed-effects linear regression estimated DYAR changes at weeks -4 through +4 relative to the injury report. Sensitivity analyses adjusted for plays per game, season week, player age, years in the league, and weeks missed.
findings: 140 concussions and 57 control injuries identified. Upon return to play (week 0 and subsequent weeks), concussed players performed at baseline DYAR — no significant decline post-injury. However, DYAR was significantly lower 2 weeks (-7.12, 95% CI -13.0 to -1.31) and 1 week (-12.2, 95% CI -17.9 to -6.5) before the injury report, suggesting pre-report performance decrement. In the week of the injury report, concussed players performed slightly better than controls (DYAR difference +11.2, p=0.06). Results were robust to sensitivity analyses.
limitations: Performance is not a guaranteed sensitive marker of neurological recovery; lingering effects may exist that do not manifest as DYAR changes. DYAR is a team-context-dependent metric that cannot fully isolate individual performance. NFL injury report diagnoses are non-specific and subject to underreporting (a 2012 poll found 56% of NFL players would hide concussion symptoms). Control group contamination (misclassified concussions) is possible. Study predates modern concussion awareness and management advances.
practicalImplications: NFL performance and medical staff can take some reassurance that the formal return-to-play protocol, as practiced during 2007-2010, was associated with players returning to baseline performance. However, the decline in performance detected 1-2 weeks before official concussion diagnosis should prompt earlier recognition efforts — sideline performance monitoring, weekly tracking of objective metrics, and low-threshold reporting culture. Performance-based metrics may complement traditional symptom-based concussion screening as an adjunct monitoring tool.
athleteDev: The multi-hit hypothesis suggested by pre-diagnosis performance declines has implications for practice design: reducing cumulative head impact exposure in practice reduces the probability of subclinical injury that may compound into diagnosed concussion. Tracking weekly objective performance metrics for skill-position players (yards per route run, completion percentage, etc.) may help identify players experiencing subclinical neurological effects before a formal concussion manifests.
rtp: Players cleared through formal concussion protocols returned to baseline DYAR performance, supporting the validity of symptom-based and neurocognitive RTP criteria for NFL skill-position players. Staff should not assume a cleared player will underperform — but should continue to monitor performance metrics and symptom recurrence in the weeks following RTP. The finding that performance declines precede official diagnosis underscores the importance of a low-threshold reporting environment and proactive monitoring throughout the season to enable earlier intervention.
